CV
  • Feb 23, 2020 Other

    Another Academic Workflow

    In past three years I had the opportunity to develop a reading/elaborating/writing workflow, which I would like to share. Before starting I want to highlight the major problems I have been encountering in my research. Eblaitology is a rather young discipline compared to other fields in Ancient History: almost nothing has been written on Ebla before its discovery in 1963.1 There has been, however, a massive research activity, in particular after the Archives came to light. Both the cuneiform documents and the scholarly publications are huge and difficult to handle: scientific publications are often scattered in hundreds of research papers written in different language. Through the bibliographic work which lead to the publication of The City of Ebla I have been able to get a grasp of all the (non-)bibliographic problems related to scientific research regarding Ebla. As for the problems related to cuneiform documents, I would not like to deal with them in this post, which is dedicated to a more general academic workflow, with particular attention to the assyriologist’s needs. So here are a few technical problems I had to solve:

    1. A way to collect and store efficiently all the references I need;
    2. A file renamer/organizer: I do not want to spend hours on time-wasting tasks like renaming PDFs and organizing them into folders, especially if the computer can do it better than me;
    3. Cross-everything: I want(ed) my work/research notes/personal files to be in such a format that I (or anyone else, if I want to share my research) can access them from any computer, regardless the operative system or the software, and also in ten (or more) years from now;
    4. I also want(ed) to archive most of my files in the cloud, preferably GDrive since it is the most cross-platform cloud service, and also offers a good working environment for a reasonable price;
    5. I want to find what I needed fast and efficiently (duh!); 1.I did not want to spend hours on formatting bibliographies. I developed a special hawk-eye (or maybe I developed a bibliographic OCD) when it comes bibliographies, and every wrong commas/space/capital letter bothers me. I am the kind of person who would spend hours just formatting the bibliography, but my PhD years taught me how valuable my time is. My computer can do this task better (and faster) than me.
    6. Open source: I prefer the pivotal elements of my workflow to be open souce, not because is free (well, maybe a little bit), but because you have complete control over the software. If I am not able to create a plugin, someone out there might have had the same issue and fixed it. Furthermore, open source softwares are stable and great for long term projects. Last but not least, they have wonderful online communities.

    So here are all the softwares/languages I use:

    Free

    • Zotero (with the following plugins: Zotfile, BetterBib(La)TeX, Zutilo)
    • Atom (with the following packages: academic-markdown, character-table, markdown-preview-enhanced, zotero-citations)
    • markdown (mostly R-flavored);
    • LaTeX and TeXMaker
    • pandoc

    Paid

    • Devonthink 3, 99-199$
    • Alfred, 25$
    • Hazel, 32$

    Tools

    Zotero

    Zotero is a well known reference management software, and probably the best out there. Contrary to mendeley and others, it is completely open-source.2 Zotero community developed several useful plugins which make Zotero the best software for managing, organizing, and exporting references collections.3

    CSL = Citation Style Language

    Before introducing other elements, let us give a look at the Citation Style Language open project. The project develops citation style language sheets with the objective of “facilitating scholarly publishing by automating the formatting of citations and bibliographies.” A CSL is a piece of code, based on XML, which describes the different formatting to be applied to a given document according to a certain citation style. The following is the initial code for the Chicago Manual of Style 17th edition:

    <?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<style xmlns="http://purl.org/net/xbiblio/csl" class="in-text" version="1.0" demote-non-dropping-particle="display-and-sort" page-range-format="chicago">
  <info>
    <title>Chicago Manual of Style 17th edition (author-date)</title>
    <id>http://www.zotero.org/styles/chicago-author-date</id>
    <link href="http://www.zotero.org/styles/chicago-author-date" rel="self"/>
    <link href="http://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/tools_citationguide.html" rel="documentation"/>
    <author>
      <name>Julian Onions</name>
      <email>julian.onions@gmail.com</email>
    </author>
  </info>

    Fortunately, all the official styles are managed by the CSL, and it is therefore only necessary to know which CSL to call in the command strings: in order to use a CSL you don’t need to speak XML. In fact Zotero, as well as many other services, heavily rely on these style sheets: these contain the guidelines for the formatting of bibliographies in all possible styles (recognized by the academic/scientific community) and supported by many softwares/markup languages. If the style you want to adopt is not mentioned among those published by the CSL Open Access repository, well, it should does not exist. The code gibberish aside, knowing how to call the right CSL will save you a lot of time since you will avoid bibliography formatting completely. All you need is a well-build Zotero library and a little bit of code.

    Markdown

    https://www.markdownguide.org

    Questions

    I need a special template/formatting when exporting to .docx via pandoc! You can call the command --template when converting the document.

    1. mentions on naram sin’s ebla and armanum ↩

    2. See for example the evaluation given in Ivey, Camille, and Janet Crum. 2018. “Choosing the Right Citation Management Tool: Endnote, Mendeley, Refworks, or Zotero.” Journal of the Medical Library Association, 106 (3): 399–403. https://doi.org/10.5195/jmla.2018.468. Note that mendeley is owned (since 2013) by Elsevier and is a freemium software: on mendeley’s controversial acquisition by Elsevier see here, here, and here. EndNote is owned by the Web of Science Group and is really not opensouce. ↩

    3. For a list of Zotero’s plugins see this page. ↩

    •    Leave a comment »
  • Feb 22, 2020 Ebla

    The City of Ebla - Supplement

    Two reference collections automatically generated using Zotero and LaTeX, unsorted or ordered by author-date. These PDFs are meant for those who prefer a printed version of the additions to on-line bibliography on the Ebla Digital Archives Project. The online searchable edition is available on the EbDA website, and regularly updated (visit ebda.cnr.it).

    For a list of the latest addition please see this page. For mistakes, errors, or omissions, please e-mail me at erica.scarpa@gmail.com.

    Permalink to this page: erica-scarpa.github.io/ebla-supplement

    Download links:

    • The City of Ebla - Supplement (sorted by author-date)
    • The City of Ebla - Supplement (unsorted)

    Last update: 2020-02-29

    EDIT #1 (2020-02-29)

    ResearchGate feature: I uploaded the two documents also on ResearchGate. Note that ResearchGate has a feature that shows all references included in a document on the “References” panel in the document view. If the document has been uploaded to ResearchGate by the author(s), this panel provides direct access to it.
    A similar option exists on academia.edu, but I have been increasingly annoyed by its click-baiting-like e-mails, and I am not going to update the page any longer, for several reasons see for example 1, 2, 3, and 4. Most of these issues affect ReasearcGate as well, but at least the latter is not spamming his users with tons of e-mails promoting paid contents subscriptions.

    Links to the documents on ResearchGate: here (unsorted) and here (author-date)
